coredata.dc:title: Cobalt oxyhydroxide and carbon dots modified by platinum as superior electrocatalyst for methanol oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Tu, Wenjing
Sun, Yue
Wu, Dan
Wang, Huibo
Huang, Hui
Shao, Mingwang
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: The engineering of electrocatalysts with high performance for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) is of great urgency for the development of direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs). Here, we designed and fabricated cobalt oxyhydroxide and carbon dots modified by platinum (Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C) hybrids with low content of Pt (13 wt%) as electrocatalyst, which exhibit high activity (856.3 mA·mg−1 Pt), stability (3 h) and better anti-poisoning ability for MOR. The Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C was synthesized by precipitate method followed with photochemical deposition. In the catalytic system, the Pt nanoparticles serve as the active center toward MOR, CoOOH-CDs use as the co-catalyst to oxidize CO-like poisonous species to CO2 on neighbouring Pt sites, and the cost-efficient carbon black enhances the conductivity for the system. The cost-efficient Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C system with improved kinetics for anodic methanol oxidation has promising application in alkaline DMFCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt oxyhydroxide
Carbon dots
Low Pt content
Electrocatalysts
Methanol oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CoOOH-CDs, CoOOH nanosheets and H2O2-CDs
Morphology and component analysis of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C catalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image; (b) HRTEM image; (c) HAADF-STEM and EDX elemental mapping images of C–K, O–K, Co–K, Co–L, Pt–L and Pt–M, (d) XRD patterns and (e) Raman (black trace) and FTIR (red trace) spectra of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C composites. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
High-resolution XPS spectra of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C: (a) Pt 4f, (b) Co 2p, (c) O 1s and (d) C 1s.

(a) CV curves of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-X % in 1 M KOH and 1 M CH3OH with scan rate of 50 mV s−1. (b) CO stripping voltammograms for Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-X % obtained in 1 M KOH. (c) Nyquist plots for Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-X %. (d) The chronoamperometric curves of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-X % in 1 M KOH and 1 M CH3OH at −0.3 V (vs. SCE). (X % = 15%, 30%, 45%, 60%, respectively.)

(a) CV curves of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C, Pt-CoOOH/C, Pt-CDs/C, Pt/C-H and Pt/C-JM in 1 M KOH and 1 M CH3OH. (b) Nyquist plots for Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C, Pt-CoOOH/C, Pt-CDs/C, Pt/C-H and Pt/C-JM. (c) The chronoamperometric curves of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C, Pt-CoOOH/C, Pt-CDs/C, Pt/C-H and Pt/C-JM in 1 M KOH + 1 M CH3OH at −0.3 V (vs. SCE). (d) CO stripping voltammograms of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C, Pt-CoOOH/C, Pt-CDs/C, Pt/C-H and Pt/C-JM in 1 M KOH.
The comparisons of peak current densities and the If/Ib values of different catalysts in 1 M KOH+1 M CH3OH.

head.ce:title: Cobalt oxyhydroxide and carbon dots modified by platinum as superior electrocatalyst for methanol oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou, 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices
Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The engineering of electrocatalysts with high performance for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) is of great urgency for the development of direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs). Here, we designed and fabricated cobalt oxyhydroxide and carbon dots modified by platinum (Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C) hybrids with low content of Pt (13 wt%) as electrocatalyst, which exhibit high activity (856.3 mA·mg−1 Pt), stability (3 h) and better anti-poisoning ability for MOR. The Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C was synthesized by precipitate method followed with photochemical deposition. In the catalytic system, the Pt nanoparticles serve as the active center toward MOR, CoOOH-CDs use as the co-catalyst to oxidize CO-like poisonous species to CO2 on neighbouring Pt sites, and the cost-efficient carbon black enhances the conductivity for the system. The cost-efficient Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C system with improved kinetics for anodic methanol oxidation has promising application in alkaline DMFCs.

Image 1
Cobalt oxyhydroxide and carbon dots modified by low content platinum (13 wt %) (Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C) as electrocatalyst for MOR.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the easily handled, stored, and transported features compared to hydrogen, methanol as an inexpensive liquid fuel has attracted more and more attention for direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) [1]. However, two major barriers prevent the large-scale commercial application of DMFCs, including slow kinetics of the anode reaction and methanol crossover through the membrane, which result both fuel loss and cell voltage decrease [2,3]. Although great efforts have been expended over the development of nonprecious-metal-based catalysts, Pt and Pt-based materials are still universally acknowledged electrocatalysts for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) due to their attractive catalytic property. However, their high cost and susceptibility against poisoning of the surface-adsorbed intermediates (CO, CHO, etc.) impede them for practical use [4].

Cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets, an emerging 2D nanomaterial, have drew much attention due to their applications in electrocatalyst [5], supercapacitor [6] as well as biosensing and imaging [7]. Especially, CoOOH has been demonstrated efficiently catalytic activity for water dissociation [8,9]. Considering the morphological features, 2D nanomaterials with high specific surface area may expose more edge-rich sites and the reaction sites. It is widely recognized that defects are conducive to produce more active sites for OHad, which would facilitate the water dissociation [10]. Notably, it is reported that the strength of OHad-Co2+δ bond in Co2+δOδ (OH)2-δ -Pt system is appropriate, neither too weak to oxidative removal CO, nor too strong to cause an adverse effect [11]. Thus, CoOOH plays a crucial role in activating water to form OHad, which then oxidizes the intermediates adsorbed on the Pt sites during the MOR.

Additionally, due to their good conductivity and the ability to improve the dispersion of metal particles, etc., carbon materials (carbon black, graphene, carbon nanotubes, etc.) have been extensively used in the design and synthesis of catalysts. More importantly, carbon dots (CDs) have been used as cocatalysts and photocatalysts in hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [12], oxygen reduction reacton (ORR) [13] and photocatalytic water splitting [14] for their easy functionalization, electron reservoir and photo-induced electron transfer, etc. In recently, the CDs have been confirmed to possess good anti-poisoning ability for MOR [15,16]. By the enlightenment of above, the combination of CoOOH and CDs with low content of Pt to design and synthesize the Pt-CoOOH-CDs hybrid, the obtained composite may be an efficient and persistent electrocatalyst for MOR.

Herein, we demonstrate cobalt oxyhydroxide and carbon dots modified by platinum (Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C) hybrid was constructed by the environment-friendly and simply precipitate method followed with photochemical deposition. By adjusting the contents of CoOOH-CDs and Pt, the optimal Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45% composites with low loading of Pt (13 wt%) exhibit efficient performance for MOR in alkaline environments with the high activity (856.3 mA mg−1 Pt), durability (3 h) and anti-poisoning property. In the catalytic system, the Pt nanoparticles serve as the active center toward MOR, CoOOH-CDs use as the co-catalyst to oxidize CO-like poisonous species to CO2 on neighbouring Pt sites, and the cost-efficient carbon black enhances the conductivity for the system.

Oxalic acid dihydrate (C2H2O4·2H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium hydroxide (KOH), chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Cobalt sulfate heptahydrate (CoSO4·7H2O), methanol (CH3OH), and Vulcan carbon powder XC-72R were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide 30% (H2O2) was bought from Yonghua Chemical Techonology Co., Ltd. The chemicals were used as received. Ultrapure water was used to prepare the solution. Deionized water and ethanol were used for washing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CoOOH-CDs, CoOOH nanosheets and H2O2-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CoOOH-CDs were synthesized according to a reported method with slight modification [17]. Typically, 2.5 mmol CoSO4·7H2O was ultrasonically dispersed in 50 ml deionized water containing 75 mg CDs (the detailed synthesis of CDs shown in Supporting Information). Then 0.05 M oxalic acid solution was added dropwise to the mixture. The precipitate was centrifuged, washed with ethanol, and dried at 65 °C. The obtained precipitate was re-dispersed in water at 45 °C, and the pH value was adjusted to about 10 with 1 M NaOH aqueous solution. Subsequently, the precipitate was placed in 8 M NaOH solution under stirring, and 6 ml 30 wt% H2O2 was added. The mixture was kept 60 °C for 16 h. Finally, the product was obtained by centrifugation, washed several times with ethanol, and dried in vacuum at 60 °C. The CoOOH nanosheet was prepared in the same process without the addition of CDs. For comparsion, the H2O2 treated CDs were prepared. The lyophilized CDs was dispersed in 8 M NaOH solution, added with 6 ml H2O2 solution and subjected to heat treatment at 60 °C for 16 h, which was defined as H2O2-CDs.

The Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C hybrids were prepared through photochemical deposition method [18]. In brief, a calculated amount of H2PtCl6 solution (38.6 mM) and Vulcan XC-72R were added into a mixture of water and methanol. Under stirring, 1 M NaOH solution was dropped until the pH value of the solution reached about 13. Then a certain amount of CoOOH-CDs was dispersed in above solution. After bubbling by N2 for 15 min, the suspension was irradiated with the Xe lamp for 5 h. The product was centrifuged, washed with ethanol and dried at 60 °C. The total mass of the samples was 100 mg. Among them, the added content of Pt is same, while the loading of CoOOH-CDs was varied from 15% to 60%. The four controlled samples were defined as Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-X%. (X% = 15%, 30%, 45%, 60%, respectively). Inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) was used to obtain the contents of Pt and Co of all the samples (Supplementary Table 1). Due to the best-performing catalyst for MOR is Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45%, the abbreviation of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C was used to replace the Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45% after comparison with CoOOH-CDs contents.

For comparison, Pt-CoOOH/C and Pt-CDs/C were synthesized with the similar procedure above, in which the as-prepared 32 mg CoOOH and 13 mg H2O2-CDs, rather than 45 mg CoOOH-CDs, were respectively added to the suspension. The home-made Pt/C (denoted as Pt/C-H) containing the same loading of Pt (13 wt%) was prepared by directly photodepositing Pt on carbon black (Vulcan XC-72R). And the commercial 20 wt % Pt/C (Pt/C-JM, Johnson Matthey Company) was used as a reference.

X-Ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted with a PIXcel3D X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean, Holland Panalytical) using Cu Kα radiation operating at 40 kV and 40 mA. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed by using Gemini 500 with acceleration voltage of 15 kV. And Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) measurements were carried out by Oxford X-Max. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were measured using a FEI Tecnai F20 (200 kV) electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected by the KRATOS Axis ultra DLD equipment with monochromatic Mg Kα X-rays (hν = 1283.3 eV). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 360 spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on an HR 800 Raman spectroscope (JY, France) with 633 nm wavelength laser light.

The catalyst ink was prepared by mixing 3 mg catalyst and 400 μL 0.5 wt % Nafion solution. After ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min, 4 μL of the ink was dropped onto the polished glassy carbon electrode (3 mm in diameter), and the work electrode was dried at room temperature. The reference and counter electrodes are saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a graphite rod, respectively. The electrochemical work station (CHI660B, Shanghai China) was used for all the electrochemical experiments.

Highly pure CO was bubbled into 1 M KOH for 30 min to form the monolayer dsorption of CO on electrocatalysts. Meanwhile, the work electrode was held at −0.65 V vs. SCE. Then the N2 was bubbling into the system for 20 min to remove the non-absorbed CO. The CO stripping voltammetry was conducted over the potential range of −1.0 V to 0.4 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

The charge integrated from the peak area of CO stripping in the first positive going sweep was corrected by subtracting the charge derived from the double layer, oxide formation during the second cycle in the same potential region. Then the electrochemical active surface (ECSA) was calculated according to the following equation:
S
CO
=
Q
CO
0.420 ×
m
Pt
Where Qco is the stripping charge, 0.420 mC cm−2 is the charge densities related with the monolayer of adsorbed CO on electrocatalysts, and mPt is the loading mass of Pt on GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and component analysis of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: In our work, the Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C was synthesized by a two-step method. In the first step, the CoOOH-CDs were synthesized by precipitate method. In the next step, the photochemical deposition method was employed to obtain the Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C. Fig. 1 a displays the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C composites. Specifically, the Pt nanoparticles appeared as the dark spots are well dispersed on the carbon black support. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) shows clear finger lattice of 0.23 nm, which is attributed to the (1 1 1) plane of Pt. And the CDs can be identified by the lattice of 0.21 nm, corresponding to its (1 0 0) plane. And the lattice spacing of 0.246 nm can be indexed to the (1 0 0) plane of CoOOH. Under scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM, Fig. 1c) and the high-annual dark-field (HAADF) images further display the sample morphology. Furthermore, the element mapping images indicate that the distribution of Co, C, O and Pt is relatively uniform.

The chemical components of the hybrids were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As illustrated in Fig. 1d, a broad peak centered at around 26° is characteristic of graphite. The diffraction peaks signed with “▲” at 39.7°, 46.3°, 67.3°81.5°, and 85.9°, are corresponding to (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 1) and (2 2 2) of Pt (JCPDS 04–0802), respectively. All the peaks marked by “■” are related to different lattice planes of CoOOH (JCPDS 72–2280).

Fig. 1e presents the Raman spectrum of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C. The I
D /I
G ratio of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C composites is about 1.17, which is slightly larger than that of H2O2-CDs (1.04, Fig. S4c). After a series of synthetic process, the disorder extent of the composites increases, causing the higher ID/IG ratio. This result suggests that there are more defects in the composites, and it is favorable to provide more active sites to promote the reaction.

For detailed comparison, a series of characterizations for CoOOH and CoOOH-CDs were performed. As shown in Fig. S1 (black line for CoOOH), the main peaks at 21.2°, 38.9°, 50.6°,61.8°, 37.0°, 69.2°, 61.8° and 78.6° are assigned to (0 0 3), (0 1 2), (0 1 5), (1 1 0), (1 0 1), (1 1 3), (1 0 7) and (2 0 2) planes of CoOOH (JCPDS 72–2280). For the CoOOH-CDs (Fig. S1, red line), the peaks show the same locations compared with the CoOOH with a bulge peak, which is due to the addition of CDs. The typical scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of CoOOH shown in Figs. S2a and S2b display that CoOOH is plate-like with broken and uneven edges. After the addition of CDs, the surfaces of CoOOH are intimately decorated with the aggregated CDs (Figs. S2c and S2d).

XPS is useful for analyzing the electronic state and surface compositions of compound. To further confirm the obtained CoOOH-CDs and CoOOH, XPS was performed. Figs. S3a and S3b exhibit the full XPS spectra of CoOOH-CDs and CoOOH, respectively, which confirm the composites include the corresponding elements. The high-resolution Co 2p XPS spectra (Figs. S3c and S3d) of the CoOOH-CDs and CoOOH display the splitting energy levels of 2p 1/2 and 2p 3/2, which are derived from the spin-orbit interaction. The two states qualitatively involve the same chemical environment information, so Co 2p 3/2 bands with higher intensity were analyzed in detail [19]. The shapes of all the curves combined with the features of satellite peak confirm Co(III) species are the essential components of CoOOH. Specifically, the main peak occurred at about 780 eV is typically characteristic of the Co(III) species [20]. And the peak at 778 eV may due to oxygen deficiency in the structure [21]. As shown in the O 1s XPS spectra of CoOOH-CDs and CoOOH (Figs. S3e and S3f), four peaks at 529.8, 531.1, 532.3 and 534.5 eV are distinctly observed, which are assigned to lattice O2− (OL), lattice OH− (OHL), adsorbed OH− (OHad) and absorbed water molecules (H2Oad) [22]. Both OL and OHL were found in the crystal structure, and their atom ratio is 1:0.94, which agrees well with the 1:1 ratio for stoichiometric CoOOH. Besides, Fig. S3g presents the curve-fitted C 1s spectrum for CoOOH-CDs. The peaks at 284.6, 285.5, 286.4, 288.6 eV are attributed to the C-C, C-OH, C-O, O-C=O, respectively [23].

CDs were prepared via electrochemical etching method reported by our group previously [24], which were further treated by H2O2. Fig. S4a displays the TEM image of H2O2-CDs. As shown, the distribution of these CDs with the average size about 2.8 nm (Fig. S4b) is homogeneous. The Raman spectrum of H2O2-CDs is shown in Fig. S4c with black line. The typical “D’’ disordered peak and ‘‘G’’ graphite peak are observed. The D peak is not always found in ideal graphite. If there are an increasing number of sp3 C-C bonds, the graphite would be disturbed gradually, and the D peak could develop rapidly. The I
D /I
G intensity ratio is an indicator of the disorder extent, as well as the proportion of sp2/sp3 bonded carbon [25]. And the I
D /I
G ratio of H2O2-CDs is 1.04. The FT-IR spectrum of H2O2-CDs (Fig. S4c with red line) indicates that CDs are rich in oxygen-containing groups, including O-H, C=O, and C-O-C. Therefore, CDs are easy functionalization.

The XPS spectra of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C are presented in Fig. 2 . The Pt 4f (7/2, 5/2) peaks are deconvoluted into three components in Fig. 2a. The peaks fitted at 71.8 and 74.9 eV are assigned to Pt0. The binding energies at 72.6 and 75.9 eV are corresponding to Pt-O. And the peaks at 74.8 and 78.3 eV are attributed to PtO2 [26]. The Co 2p shake-up structure is generally used to distinguish different oxidation states of colbat [14,20]. Compared with the Co 2p spectrum of CoOOH-CDs (Fig. S3c), an additional satellite peak appeared at 786.3 eV represents the formation of some Co(II) after the photo-deposition reaction (Fig. 2b). Moreover, the O 1s spectrum illustrates the ratio of OL to OHL is 1:1.4. Although the Co(II) constitutes a small proportion on the surface, the primary bulk material is still CoOOH, which has been determined by XRD. The high-resolution XPS of O 1s and C 1s (Fig. 2c and d) for Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C are similar with those of the CoOOH-CDs. In addition, the XPS spectra for all the samples are shown in Figs. S5–S8. The EDS spectrum of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45% displays in Fig. S9.

The catalytic activities of the Pt-CoOOH-CD/C-X % for methanol oxidation were tested in 1 M KOH and 1 M CH3OH. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the Pt-CoOOH-CD/C-45% catalyst display the lowest onset potential, which is about −0.60 V, demonstrating its preferable electrocatalytic activity. And among the four hybrids, the Pt-CoOOH-CD/C-45% catalyst exhibits the highest mass-normalized current density (856.3 mA·mg−1 Pt) in the positive going scan. Therefore, the optimal loading of CoOOH-CDs is 45 wt%.

As illustrated in Fig. S10a, the anodic peak at about −0.3 V (vs. SCE) is assigned to the generation of the platinum oxide during the forward scan [27]. While a small oxidation peak observed at −0.1 to 0.0 V is related to the transformation of Co(II) to Co(III), and the peak at 0.1–0.2 V is indexed to the oxidation of Co(III) to Co(IV) [28]. Furthermore, the hydrogen desorption peak over the potential range −0.9∼-0.7 V gradually decreases as the content of CoOOH-CDs increases. According to a prior report [11], Pt modified by 3d-M hydr(oxy)oxide would reduce the surface coverage of adsorbed hydrogen (Hupd). And they could selectively hinder the adsorption of Hupd without affecting the Pt–Hupd energetics. Hence, the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) estimated here is not based on the hydrogen desorption charge, while calculated from the CO stripping experiments (Fig. S11). Among the four samples, Pt-CoOOH-CD/C-45% hybrids have the largest value, which is about 63.64 m2 g−1. The peak potential of CO absorption was used to determinate the anti-poisoning ability of methanol oxidation catalysts [29,30]. It can be seen that the Pt-CoOOH-CD/C-45% hybrids exhibit the most negative peak potential, indicating the best CO tolerance (Fig. 3b).

In addition, the electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) was obtained at −0.4 V to evaluate the kinetics of methanol oxidation [31]. The impedance behaviors are distinct under different potentials, which are associated with the reaction mechanism and rate-determining steps. Methanol dehydrogenation is the main controlled step at low potential, while the oxidative removal of carbonaceous poison becomes the dominance at high potential [32]. Here, the EIS data at intermediate potential region (−0.4 V) from the methanol dehydrogenation to oxidative removal of CO is chosen to compare the activity of the four catalysts. It can be seen that a positive loop in the high frequencies is accompanied with an inductive tail in low frequency range in the Nyquist plots (Fig. 3c), which is the typical feature in the transition region [31,33]. The equivalent circuit of impedance based on the EIS curves is shown as an inset in Fig. 3c, in which the Rs, Rp, and Rct respresent the solution resistance, pore resistance and charge transfer resistance, respectively. And the Qdl is a constant phase element (CPE) representing the double layer, which is caused by the microscopic roughness. And such roughness would result in the inhomogeneous distribution in the solution resistance and in the double-layer capacitance [34]. The experiment results are displayed as data plots, and the fitted curves based on the equivalent circuit are drew as the solid lines. The diameter of the semicircle is related with the charge transfer resistance (Rct). The value of the diameter for the Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45% is smaller than the other, indicating the faster reaction rate [35,36].

The durability is another important factor to evaluate electrochemical performance. Fig. 3d displays the i-t curves of the four catalysts at constant potential −0.3V versus. SCE for 3 h. All the catalysts show a rapid decay in the initial period, which stems from the formation of intermediate species, such as CO and CHO. While the inevitable poisoning continues over time, the catalysts exhibit slow activity loss [37–39]. After continuously operating for 3 h, the Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-15%, −30%, −45%, −60% retain 17.0%, 42.7%, 65.24%, 48.08% of their original current densities, respectively. The Pt-CoOOH-CD/C-45% catalyst presents the highest residual current after 3 h as well. Fig. S12 shows the TEM images of the Pt-CoOOH-CD/C-45% before and after stability test. No obvious agglomeration of Pt particles was observed after 3 h operation, indicating the better CO tolerance and durability of the catalyst.

Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45% exhibits higher performance than that of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-15%, −30%, −60% contrastive samples. When the content of CoOOH-CDs is below 45%, the composites couldn't provide enough active oxygen to remove the carbonaceous intermediate species. While the composites with higher CoOOH-CDs proportion, redundant hydroxide species may adsorb on the surface of electrocatalysts, and then block the hydrogen adsorption/desorption. The active sites of the composites were covered [40]. Therefore, Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45% with appropriate content of co-catalysts exhibits the highest performance than that of others contrastive samples.

In the following experiments, to further comprehend the electrochemical behaviours of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45% composites, the Pt-CoOOH/C, Pt-CDs/C, and Pt/C-H were synthesized for comparsion, and the Pt/C-JM purchased from Johnson Matthey Company was also used as a reference (in order to facilitate comparison, the Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45% was abbreviated for Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C). The four catalysts (Pt-CoOOH/C, Pt-CDs/C, Pt/C-H and Pt/C-JM) were characterized by XRD and TEM (Figs. S14 and S15). Fig. 4 a depicts the CV curves of four catalysts in N2-saturated 1 M KOH with 1 M CH3OH. The peak current of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C in the positive scan is about 856.3 mA mg−1 Pt, which is 1.97-folder and 1.52-folder higher than those of Pt-CoOOH/C and Pt-CDs/C, respectively. The possible reason is that CDs anchored with CoOOH could improve the interface properties, accelerating the charge transfer from the catalysts to the substrate. Besides, the synergistic effects among the different components enhance the mass activity. Furthermore, the peak current density of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C is about 1.43 times as active as the Pt/C-JM, 1.63 times as activity as Pt/C-H. It's noted that the anodic peaks in the forward scan (If) is ascribe to the oxidation of freshly adsorbed methanol, while the anodic peak in the reverse scan (Ib) is attribute to the removal of the incompletely oxidized intermediate species [41]. The ratio of the forward peak current to the backward one (If/Ib) was used to evaluate the resistance ability of Pt-based catalyst to poisonous carbonaceous products [42,43]. As listed in Table 1 , the If/Ib value of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C is the highest, suggesting its better CO resistance. The presence of CoOOH-CDs might facilitate methanol dehydrogenation, and the interaction between Pt and CoOOH-CDs accelerates the process.

From the EIS data in Fig. 4b, the charge transfer resistances are in the order of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C < Pt/C-JM < Pt-CDs/C < Pt/C-H < Pt-CoOOH/C, indicating the incorporation of CoOOH-CDs could lead to much faster electron transfer rate, and hence improving the catalytic activity. And considering the i-t curves (Fig. 4c), the catalysts present the similar decay periods. At the end of 10800 s, the Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C catalyst remains 273 mA·mg−1 Pt, much higher than the other composites. The initial current density of Pt-CDs/C is lower than Pt/C-JM, which is consistent with the data in CV curves for MOR. However, Pt-CDs/C exhibits superior stability by delivering a higher current density after 3 h (159.3 mA·mg−1 Pt) compared with Pt/C-JM (117 mA·mg−1 Pt). The improved stability may be ascribed to the synergistic coupling between Pt and CDs [39]. Similarly, the Pt-CoOOH/C displays a relative slow decay and higher final current densities in reference to Pt/C-H.

CO stripping voltammetry (Fig. 4d) was conducted to further explain the CO tolerance and estimate the ECSA. Two oxidation peaks are observed at both Pt/C-H and Pt/C-JM between −0.5 V and −0.2 V (vs. SCE), attributing to the fact that CO is oxidized at different lattices of Pt particles [44]. Not surprising, the Pt-CDs/C catalyst shows a negatively shift of the peak potential, which might attribute to the functional groups rich in CDs, especially hydroxyls and carboxyl groups, weaken the binding energy of CO at Pt sites, making it easier for CO electroxidation [15]. As for Pt-CoOOH/C catalyst, a new intense peak emerges at less positive potential from −0.6 V to −0.5 V, probably due to CO oxidation at Pt sites near CoOOH. According to the mechanism proposed in previous literature [10,11], CO is adsorbed solely on the Pt sites, the OHad species adsorb preferentially on 3d-M hydr(oxy)oxides, facilitating the oxidative removal of CO. Notably, when CDs were introduced in the synthesis process of CoOOH, the as-prepared Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C catalyst shows the most negative peak potential located at around −0.59 V. CoOOH and the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs work together with Pt to enhance the anti-poisoning property. For further prominent the performance of Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C in MOR, we have summarized the current electrocatalysts for comparsion shown Table S2. As shown, the Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C shows the high-performance as electrocatalyst for MOR in alkaline media.

We develop a cost-effective Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C electrocatalyst with high-performance for MOR in alkaline media. The Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C composites were constructed by the environment-friendly and simply precipitate method followed with photochemical deposition. During the design process, the CoOOH-CDs and Pt as construction units were employed. By adjusting the contents of CoOOH-CDs and Pt, the optimal Pt-CoOOH-CDs/C-45% composite with low loading of Pt (13 wt%) exhibits high activity (856.3 mA mg−1 Pt), durability (3 h) and anti-poisoning property in MOR. In the catalytic system, the Pt nanoparticles serve as the active center toward MOR; CoOOH-CDs act as the co-catalyst to oxidize CO-like poisonous species to CO2 on neighbouring Pt sites and the cost-efficient carbon black enhances the conductivity for the system. And the obtained electrocatalyst is of great potential in alkaline direct methanol fuel cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 51572179, 21471106, 21771132, 21501126, 21771134), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161216), the 111 project and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
